food_subgroup: Pasta dishes with poultry and sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken pesto pasta; FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pasta dishes broadly; no specific code for chicken pesto pasta exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for chicken pesto pasta; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of chicken pesto pasta is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of pasta tossed with pesto sauce and sliced or diced chicken breast.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta with pesto sauce and chicken — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entries: Pasta, cooked (FDC ID 20420); Chicken breast, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 05064); Pesto sauce, commercially prepared (FDC ID 172949)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for chicken pesto pasta as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted blend of cooked pasta (~55% by weight), cooked chicken breast (~25% by weight), and commercially prepared pesto sauce (~20% by weight), using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for each component. Proportions are representative of a typical restaurant preparation. Energy cross-checked against published recipe analyses.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked, al dente) GI approximately 40–50; composite dish estimated at GI 45
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken pesto pasta as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 based on the dominant carbohydrate source (cooked pasta), which has a well-established GI of 40–50 (low category) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The protein and fat content from chicken and pesto further attenuate the glycemic response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 68.25) / 100 ≈ 30.7 g (serving carbohydrate: 19.5 g/100g × 350 g = 68.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~1085 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a standard 350 g restaurant serving of chicken pesto pasta provides approximately 1085 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from pesto sauce and Parmesan cheese; requesting reduced-cheese or sauce-on-the-side preparations can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~30.7 g per serving) despite low GI pasta base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the pasta base carries a low glycemic index (~45), the large portion size yields a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving (~30.7 g); people with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion (150–200 g), choose wholegrain pasta where available, and pair the dish with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from Parmesan cheese and olive oil; sodium load from pesto
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken pesto pasta contains olive oil (a source of cardioprotective monounsaturated fats) but also Parmesan cheese contributing saturated fat (~1.8 g/100 g) and significant sodium; individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, opt for reduced-cheese preparations, and ensure the dish is part of an overall diet low in saturated fat and sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken pesto pasta is a popular Italian-inspired pasta dish consisting of cooked pasta — typically spaghetti, penne, or fusilli — tossed with a vibrant green basil pesto sauce and tender pieces of cooked chicken breast. The pesto sauce is traditionally made by blending fresh basil leaves, pine nuts, garlic, Parmesan cheese, and extra-virgin olive oil, yielding a rich, herbaceous, and slightly nutty flavour profile. The chicken is usually grilled, pan-seared, or poached before being sliced and combined with the pasta and sauce. The dish has a creamy-yet-light texture, with the olive oil providing a silky coating and the Parmesan adding umami depth. Nutritionally, a standard restaurant serving (~350 g) provides approximately 648 kcal, 40 g protein, 25 g fat, and 68 g carbohydrate. The dish is a moderate source of B vitamins (particularly niacin and B6 from chicken), iron, and phosphorus. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), driven by the pasta base, though the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~30.7 g) due to portion size. Clinically, the dish is relevant for sodium management in hypertension (pesto and cheese contribute ~1085 mg sodium per serving), glycemic control in Type 2 Diabetes (moderate GL warrants portion awareness), and cardiovascular health (olive oil provides MUFAs but saturated fat from cheese should be monitored). The dish appears on menus across Singapore, including at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants that offer international pasta options alongside their core cuisine.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, USDA) contains a direct entry for chicken pesto pasta as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (cooked pasta, cooked chicken breast, commercially prepared pesto sauce). GI is estimated from the analogous pasta GI range per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no single approved source entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component source entries and is not a characteristic nutrient of this dish. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients (pasta, chicken breast, olive-oil-based pesto) — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked pasta GI (40–50, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chicken pesto pasta as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. The food appears on the field survey data menu of Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine (Boat Quay), indicating it is served as an international menu item outside its Italian origin context. 6. Halal status is marked false as pesto traditionally contains Parmesan cheese (which may use animal rennet) and the restaurant context does not confirm halal certification for this specific dish. 7. The cuisine tag in the input JSON lists 'Turkish, Kebab, Shawarma' — these reflect the restaurant's primary cuisine, not the dish itself; this record correctly classifies the dish as Italian-inspired/International. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are provided as best-available estimates rather than direct measurements.
